speaker: interviewee
text: So I was taken to the flitzer cell, to the place where the escapees were. And there I met another gentleman that I did not know, but he was from Brussels also, and he was a real escapee. Because in 1943, the Jewish underground attacked a train that was going from Mechelen to Germany. They attacked a train, and some people were killed. There was shooting between the German guards of the train and the Jewish underground. And that's was right before Passover 1943. And the niece of this particular man-- his name was Silbershwartz-- his niece was killed in the escape. But it was a grand escape. Maybe 200 or 300 people escaped from that train, and he was one of them. He was caught in a hiding place a year later. They had him on the record that he escaped. And he was put-- he was my neighbor, and we became very friendly with-- with-- we're together. The-- the commander-- the Jewish commander of the camp, the lagerfuhrer, Mayer, continued to protect me. The letter came from the headquarters and the Gestapo that I should do the dirtiest work in camp. The dirtiest work, what he gave me to do, was, you know, in the-- during the night, people to go to the bathroom. So they had in the each hole, they had a big, a kind of, of a pot, of a bottle, huge. And that was for 100 people, for whatever they had to do during the night. I was ordered to take these out from each of the holes, and to take it out and take care of it. That was my job and punishment. But it was a pretty good exchange for the bunker. I did it for-- for a while, for a few days, then he took me off and he gave me a job in the washroom. There was a big washroom, big enough for 1,000 people to wash in the morning. And my job together with other prisoners was to clean that washroom. That job did not give any privileges to these prisoners. There was no privilege like to escape a transport or anything. We were just like anybody else, but we had to wash the washroom. The reason he gave it to me is because I had a red band, and as I work in the washroom, I got a white band, which said the washroom [INAUDIBLE]. And he told me not to be too visible in the courtyard, so I should put the white band on top of the red band.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the white band bigger than the red band?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was a little wider.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I did that. And also, the [? flitzers ?] had to be, the head had to be shaved like every 10 days or two weeks, whatever, very short periods so they should always keep a very bald head, so they should be visible. He told me if-- to try to avoid when the barber comes, I should hide. And I did, and I had little hair.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then three weeks later-- what I want to mention also-- is that at that time, you know, the Allies were starting to come closer to the invasion. There was a lot of bombing and-- all over Western Europe. Allied bombers also bombed Mechelen because Mechelen was a-- an important railroad center. There were railroad-- huge railroad yards. And they were bombing these railroad yards. And many of the bombs did not explode. Some of them were-- maybe they were defective. Other bombs did not explode because they had a time clock, and they would explode in 10 hours, or 12 hours or whatever. So this way, they kept them from repairing the-- the-- the railroad yards. They came-- they came to the camp and they ordered-- no. First, they wanted volunteers. They wanted 25 volunteers, Jews, to dig out the unexploded bombs. I did not volunteer, but they took the people with the red bands-- there were quite a few-- they ordered them be amongst that group that would go and dig up bombs. We did that work for about two weeks. And the commander said, this is very dangerous work, and because you do this work, all the people that do this work will not be deported. We went every day. We were picked up by the [NON-ENGLISH], that was the air force. Soldiers and officers from the air force took us to the places. They showed us where to dig, they gave us the tools, the picks, and we were digging around-- around the-- the bombs. There were big bombs, from 250 kilos and 500 kilos. We had to make a big hole all around the bomb, the-- dig out the entire bomb. Then the-- the air force officers, engineers would come. They would put ropes around, and take a crane, and pull it out and detonate. But these bombs had live detonators, so when we were digging them, these bombs could explode at any time. And the guards from the air force that where guarding us stayed like 500 meters away from the place where we were digging.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were 25 and five escaped because it was very easy. We could have all escaped. I could've escaped too, but my family was in Mechelen, and they would take revenge on them. So I did not escape.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I must say that the two or three weeks that we were digging the bombs and we were under the air force, we were very well-treated. There was no beating. And a matter of fact, I talked to my guard, who was a middle-aged man from Hamburg. Told me he had a lot of Jewish clients, he was a coal dealer. And I told him that when we were in hiding, we were listening to the BBC. Even so, it was jammed, but we listened to it in different languages, in Polish. And we knew from the BBC they were telling us that they were killing, exterminating all the Jews in Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when I told this to my guard, he told me, you believe that? You believe that we could do something like that? That's propaganda, says, from the British. That's not true. Nobody's killing you. I believed that he really didn't believe it himself. He didn't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were working with these bombs, and nothing happened, and we were taken back to Mechelen. And of course, there was no privilege for anyone. Everybody was deportable. All the people that took part in the bombing, in the detail to dig the bombs, were deported. Now we're coming back to the certificates. The-- the certificate, that list on which my brother put us two years before when we were in hiding, all of a sudden became an important thing. We didn't remember the whole story, why where in Mechelen. My sister, as I said before, escaped through the roofs, and she-- she got out. They were-- the Gestapo posted a detail in the street for my sister, they had her picture from the identity card. But the people where she-- where she went down into the house through the roof-- which were total strangers, and they were good people-- and they told her to dress as a man. They gave her a man's suit and a hat. They were looking for a woman, and she was dressed as a man, and that's how she escaped that area, that neighborhood, where they were looking for her. And she went into hiding with another sister. Anyway, coming back to-- to the camp, so my sister was outside, and she had the opportunity to find out things and to send us food into the camp. They-- from the clandestine Palestinian office, the head of that office, was a name-- his name was Szatan. He was from the leftist Zionist movement, and he was himself, on that list. He was outside, and he got in touch with my sister, or she got in touch with him, and he told that we are on the list, and that the list has a value. A few days before the transport, which-- which was the 26th transport from Mechelen-- each transport had about 1,000 Jews-- we was scheduled for the 26th transport. A few days before the transport, my sister succeed to tell the Judenrat that we are on the Palestinian list. The-- in order to have a value, you-- there were three steps that had to be accomplished. First, you had to get a letter from the Palestinian office in Geneva that you are on the list, which was a [? fake ?] letter that from a Jewish institution. That letter was good enough to be postponed, to skip one transport. We got that letter a few days before the transport, and that's what gave us the chance to be what they called [NON-ENGLISH], postponed for the next transport. I remember Mayer, the Jewish kapo, came. Before the transport, he went into each hold with a list of the people that have postponed for the next transport. And I was looking out the window, I remember, to see if Mayer is coming towards the quarters of the [? flitzers, ?] of the escapees. Each hall had what they called a [GERMAN]. He was-- he was-- the head of the-- he was running the-- the hall. He was the-- he was the leader, he was the chief. I remember my [GERMAN]. His name was Gingold. We had very few people in our hall. There were only maybe six or seven. And he himself was not an escapee. He was there from the beginning. He was there because he was married to a non-Jewish-- his wife was not Jewish. And she somehow worked for him that he should stay in the camp, he shouldn't be deported. He was an elderly man. He saw me looking at-- at courtyard if Mayer is coming. He told me, listen. I don't want you to be disappointed. But I'm here for two years in this escapee. I'm the leader of this room. And I want you to know that it never happened that a [? flitzer, ?] an escape, should skip a transport, should be put for-- for a-- for another transport. That doesn't exist. So make-- don't make yourself any illusions. You'll be deported.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Mayer did not come to our room, to the escapee room, he didn't come because it was an impossibility. But Mayer called out my name in my parents' room that I am on the list. And I was on the list, and so was my friend, Silbershwartz. He also got that letter. And we both were not deported. The transport went without us. When the transport left, we had a roll call. We had roll call every day, and there were maybe 20 people left from 1,000. And the commander, Frank, asked to the-- to Mayer, he asked why these two [? flitzers, ?] why weren't they put into train? So Mayer said, they have the Palestinian papers. He-- he got a very angry face, and like-- very-- he was-- his anger-- his face was full of anger. He didn't say nothing. He walked away. And we stayed. We stayed-- we stayed until-- I'll not go in detail the life that was there. There were many things happening.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were Dutch Jews committing suicide, people were hanging themselves. They-- I had met a lot of Dutch Jews that were prominent Jews in Holland. One of them was also on the list. And I-- I'll make it short to the next transport. The next transport was a very brutal transport. Because a few days before the transport, a delegate from [? Aikemen ?] came, which I remember him standing in the courtyard. And he ordered the camp to be liquidated. That was already in August, 1944. The invasion was on. I also skipped one episode that we were taking 40 of us from the camp. To-- there was-- there was a training-- an SS training camp on the Dutch border. The town was called Schoten. And we were taking the 40 Jews because there were SS from all over Europe in that camp, and they wanted to have a sample of Jews, how to torture Jews. So the brought from our camp for the Jews to that SS training camp to teach them how to deal with Jews. So it was no picnic to stay there. We stayed there until the invasion of June 6. June 6, they wanted to shoot us, but somehow, they didn't. And we were taking back to Mechelen. That camp was liquidated. They all went to the front to Normandy. And that by itself is a long episode, which could take a long time to tell. But I won't stay on on this. The next transport went in August. The-- in the meantime, they've caught my sister, my brother-in-law and my niece, and they were brought to Mechelen also. And my-- my niece was very lucky. They took her out, and she was placed in a children's camp, and she survived the war. My brother-in-law and my sister were not on that Palestinian list. My brother-in-law was deported, then he was killed, never came back. My sister was in Auschwitz, but she came back. She survived the-- the war. The-- the last transport, that was the 27th transport-- the paper from the Palestinian office was not sufficient. You had to have a paper, a confirming paper, from the International Red Cross, with a number on which list you are, and with-- which number and which number list exactly. It had to be very detailed if you're really on the list for exchange. And my friend, Silbershwartz, did not get that communication from the Red Cross in time, he was deported. They also deported almost the entire Jewish personnel from the camp, which was there for two years. It was a very brutal transport. There were a lot of screams and beatings, and it was-- it was terrible. Matter of fact, that was the last transport. We got the confirmation, like the miracle, we got the confirmation from the Red Cross, with a number, with everything, just a few days before that 26th transport. That was 20-- 27th transport. And again, I was put-- I was postponed for the next transport. I got the new transport number.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at that time, I was the only [? flitzer, ?] the only escapee, left. And Mayer had a problem. How can you keep one escapee in the hall? The-- the head of the hall, the leader, the [GERMAN], was deported. Everybody was deported. So he told me there is no other way. You leave the escapee place, the [? flizter ?] [? is ?] [? out. ?] You go together with your family, and forget that you are [? flitzer. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: The day of the transport he said, make that you don't exist, hide somewhere. Because if they'll find you, yes papers or no papers, they'll pack you up. Hide, don't be in their eyes. The Germans shouldn't see you. After the transport will leave, go in the room with your parents, you-- forget. You no [? flitzer. ?] And that's what I did. After that last transport left, there were very few Jews left. There was only one-- one hall, and I went together with my parents. But the transport left sometimes in August and they were-- even saw that they had the-- the war was lost for them. The Allies were advancing in France. They broke out from Normandy. They were still bring in Jews every day. What they do at the end, they brought the Jews from the Judenrat. They were going to deport them. They brought in the-- brought in the chief rabbi with his family. And they tried to get whatever Jews were officially covered by them, that worked somewhere, in old age homes. They rounded up all these Jews, and they brought them to camp. And towards the end, we had a-- we were, again, a total of about 500 Jews. On September the 2nd, they-- they assembled all the Jews in the courtyard. They put a table with a machine gun on the table. And they sat down, and SS manning the machine gun. The commander ordered everybody should do-- should be at the courtyard. At that time, Allied planes were flying over the roof all the time. They-- we knew that it's the end. Even so, we had no radio. We found out news through different sources. Through the Germans, through the SS. We knew that they are in Paris, that they are approaching Brussels, and that is coming to the end. The day-- day before, the entire Gestapo from Brussels and from Northern France rolled into our courtyard. A caravan of trucks and cars, a few hundred of them. SS, all the murderers, they came to us. And so the day before, on September the 2nd, he-- we were all on courtyard, and the commander said he wants this Frank. He wants to speak. He-- he went on the table and he said, I can make it fairly simple. As you see, this machine-- this machine gun is set to kill you all. In few minutes, said none of you would be left. But I'm not going to do it, he said, because when the enemy will come in, he'll make propaganda out of this. Apparently, he was ordered to do it, that's what we found out later. But he disobeyed the order, and he didn't. On the night of September the 3rd, around midnight, they all left. They opened the gates, and the entire caravan-- all the cars, all the trucks, they all left the camp to our disbelief. We couldn't believe it. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Wait a second. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They-- they left the camp, and there were no Germans in the camp. The Jews got together-- anyway, most of them or maybe all of them-- they all came to our hall, and we organized a service, a religious service. And we prayed. And we said, you know, Hallel. You know, Hallel you only said-- say on big holidays. On very important holidays, we say Hallel, which is thanksgiving. So we didn't hesitate for one minute that this is the biggest holiday of all the Jewish holidays, and we said Hallel. And people came that never prayed in their life, everybody was there. A lot of people cried, it was very emotional. And after this, we danced. Went around, all around, all around like a dance at the Jewish wedding. We were singing and dancing for a long time. Then we went to sleep. We put a few guys on guard on the outside. And in the morning, we woke up very early, very early. As soon as the daybreak, we started to move out of the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want you to know that we got out of the camp, there was no reception committee outside to-- to receive us, to give us a welcome. What we found outside was maybe a number-- maybe 100 to 200 people, Belgians, with-- with the sacks to loot-- to loot from the camp if there was anything to loot there. What was there to loot from these poor Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, that's only a detail. We walked out of the camp, and we started to walk in the streets. As we walked out from camp, we did-- there was a Jewish committee came already from Brussels. And because we had no money, and they gave everybody 1,000 or 2,000 francs so we should have money to take a bus, or a train or whatever. There were no buses, there were no trains, there was nothing. Walked out in the streets, there were no Germans and there were no Allies. There was nobody. The underground was running in the streets. During the war, you couldn't see much of them. But once the Germans were gone, everybody became underground. They all put on bands say underground. All had their rifles, and their running from cars, looking for Germans. Through the war, we saw very little of them. And we-- we walked in the streets for a few hours. Not too long, I think was like 10 o'clock, and people started to scream that the British-- [INAUDIBLE]-- [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] That the British are there. Not to block their way, they are British. And we were in-- a block away, and we saw the British. We saw the British tanks, was a column of tanks. And we-- we stopped the tanks, and we told them that they liberated a camp with 500 Jews. They say, oh, very interesting. We're going to-- we're going to call headquarters, and we go and tell them over the radio. But we-- we kissed the tanks. We kissed the metal. And-- I have to wait a second.
